headings.text: Five years on the forge floor.

paragraphs.text: We believe icons are quiet workhorses of an interface. Phosphor Glyph Forge is a five-year experiment in giving them the craft, weight, and consistency they deserve — without losing the warmth of a hand-drawn line.

Phosphor Glyph Forge began as a side project in a Brooklyn studio in late 2019 — a small set of 36 duotone icons drawn to solve one team's product nav bar. Five years on, it has grown into 400+ glyphs used in nineteen countries.

Every glyph in the library passes through the same three hands. We draft on a strict 24-pixel grid, refine on a 48-pixel preview, and only ship after a glyph survives at least eleven real product placements. There are no algorithmically generated shapes here — and never will be.
Every milestone below was a release, a redraw, or a rethink — never a marketing beat.
Every path begins on a Wacom tablet. No AI fill, no vector parametrics — just hand-tuned anchors on a 24-pixel grid.
Duotone is more than a look — it's hierarchy. The foreground carries the verb, the back layer carries the object.
Icons live next to verbs and nouns. They should never compete with them — they should make the reading faster.
Phosphor Glyph Forge is dual-licensed: MIT for individual products, FORGE-COMMERCIAL for redistributing teams. Read the full terms in licensing.md.
400+ hand-crafted duotone glyphs, one disciplined grid, and a manifesto we still believe in.

items.text:
Principle 01
      Drawn, not generated.
      Every path begins on a Wacom tablet. No AI fill, no vector parametrics — just hand-tuned anchors on a 24-pixel grid.

Principle 02
      Two layers, one meaning.
      Duotone is more than a look — it's hierarchy. The foreground carries the verb, the back layer carries the object.

Principle 03
      Quiet by default.
      Icons live next to verbs and nouns. They should never compete with them — they should make the reading faster.

Phosphor Glyph ForgeEST. 2019 · FORGED IN PIXELS
      
      400+ hand-crafted duotone glyphs, one disciplined grid, and a manifesto we still believe in.

We believe icons are quiet workhorses of an interface. Phosphor Glyph Forge is a five-year experiment in giving them the craft, weight, and consistency they deserve — without losing the warmth of a hand-drawn line. Origin Story A library shaped by use. Phosphor Glyph Forge began as a side project in a Brooklyn studio in late 2019 — a small set of 36 duotone icons drawn to solve one team's product nav bar. Five years on, it has grown into 400+ glyphs used in nineteen countries. Every glyph in the library passes through the same three hands. We draft on a strict 24-pixel grid, refine on a 48-pixel preview, and only ship after a glyph survives at least eleven real product placements. There are no algorithmically generated shapes here — and never will be. The people behind the forge Mira Halverson FOUNDER · DRAFTING Theo Iwasaki CO-FOUNDER · SYSTEM Aanya Rao ICONOGRAPHER · REVIEW Lukas Brandt ENGINEERING · TOOLING “A good icon doesn't ask to be looked at — it asks to be understood at a glance, and then politely vanishes.” — Mira Halverson, founding designer 2019 → 2024 Five years on the forge floor. Duotone is more than a look — it's hierarchy. The foreground carries the verb, the back layer carries the object. Principle 03 Quiet by default. Icons live next to verbs and nouns. They should never compete with them — they should make the reading faster.
